items.question_text: What is the permitted basis for airport traffic control service?

choices.text: Observed or known traffic and airport conditions only.
Expected traffic and likely airport conditions when the controller is confident the situation is routine.
Pilot assumptions about where nearby traffic probably is when no conflict has been reported.
General pattern expectations even when the traffic cannot be observed or confirmed.

items.explanation: Airport traffic control service is based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

items.question_text: Provide airport traffic control service based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the controlling directive in 3-1-1.

items.question_text: Traffic information is used in Class B, Class C, or Class D surface areas, in terminal radar service areas, and for transiting aircraft operating near terminal locations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note explains where traffic information is especially useful around terminal locations.

items.instruction: Is this traffic-information capability statement correct?
items.question_text: Traffic information provides collision-avoidance help to pilots near terminal locations, but it will not remove the pilot's responsibility to avoid collision under the CFRs.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note keeps pilot collision-avoidance responsibility in place even when ATC traffic information is provided.

items.situation: A VFR pilot near the Class D surface area asks whether another aircraft is "no factor." You know one aircraft's position, but the other aircraft has not been observed and its exact location is unknown. Another controller says to reassure the pilot anyway because the pattern is usually predictable.
items.para_context: Airport traffic control service is based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions. Traffic information can aid pilots, but pilots remain responsible for avoiding collisions.

choices.text: Provide only traffic information based on what is observed or known and do not reassure the pilot based on assumptions.
Advise that the other aircraft is no factor because the traffic pattern is normally predictable.
Withhold all traffic information because collision avoidance is always solely the pilot's responsibility.
Assume the unknown aircraft is outside the surface area unless the pilot reports it in sight.

items.explanation: Controllers work from observed or known conditions, not assumptions, while still giving useful traffic information when they have it.

items.question_text: When may preventive control service be provided?

choices.text: Only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement.
To any aircraft in the movement area whenever routine approvals would slow the operation.
Only when tower radar is in use and the controller can watch every aircraft continuously.
Whenever the pilot requests fewer controller instructions during pattern work.

items.explanation: Preventive control service is limited to aircraft operating in accordance with an LOA or its DoD equivalent.

items.question_text: Provide preventive control service only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The service is not general tower practice; it is limited to LOA-governed operations.

items.question_text: When providing preventive control service, you must issue advice or instructions only if a situation develops that requires corrective action.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Preventive control removes routine approvals and keeps intervention for developing conflicts that need correction.

items.question_text: Airfield operating instructions, memorandums of understanding, or other directives used exclusively by the Department of Defense can satisfy the agreement requirement for preventive control service.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note expressly recognizes certain DoD-only directives as satisfying the LOA criteria.

items.situation: Two aircraft are operating under an LOA that allows preventive control service. The pattern is stable and no conflict is developing. Another controller wants to continue issuing routine turn approvals and repeated advisories on every leg just to keep tight control of the operation.
items.para_context: Preventive control eliminates repetitious routine approval of pilot action. Controllers intervene only when a situation develops that requires corrective action.

choices.text: Do not issue repetitive routine approvals; monitor the operation and intervene only if a developing situation requires corrective action.
Keep issuing routine approvals on every leg because preventive control means closer-than-normal controller involvement.
Suspend the LOA operation immediately because preventive control forbids any controller intervention at all.
Convert the operation to ordinary preventive control for any aircraft in the pattern, even if they are not covered by the LOA.

items.explanation: Preventive control is narrower and lighter-touch than normal tower control, but it still requires intervention when a conflict develops.

items.question_text: Before ground control authorizes an aircraft or vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway, what must happen?

choices.text: Ground control must obtain approval from local control, and the coordination must include the point or intersection at the runway.
Ground control may authorize the crossing first if the pilot or vehicle says the runway appears clear.
The airport manager must close the runway temporarily unless local control can watch the entire movement visually.
No coordination is needed when the runway crossing will be brief and the vehicle is already on the movement area frequency.

items.explanation: Local control has primary responsibility for the active runway, so ground must get approval and specify where the movement will occur.

items.question_text: Ground control must obtain local-control approval before authorizing an aircraft or vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway, and the coordination must include the point or intersection involved.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the core positive-coordination requirement for active-runway use.

items.question_text: The ground controller must advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete, either verbally or through visual aids specified by facility directive.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Positive coordination includes closing the loop when the runway operation is finished.

items.question_text: If an aircraft or vehicle will taxi or proceed on or along an active runway for a purpose other than crossing, authorization must normally be provided by direct communications on the appropriate local-control frequency.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: For on-runway use other than crossing, authorization is normally on the local frequency, with a facility-directive exception after coordination.

items.situation: A vehicle on the ground-control frequency needs to cross Runway 27 at Alpha. Another controller says, "Local already knows the vehicle is moving around the airport, so just tell it to cross and let them figure out where."
items.para_context: Local control has primary responsibility for active-runway operations. Ground must obtain approval before authorizing a crossing or any runway use, and the coordination must include the runway point or intersection. Ground must advise local when the operation is complete.

choices.text: Obtain local-control approval that includes the specific runway point, issue the crossing only after that coordination, and advise local when the operation is complete.
Issue the crossing immediately because the vehicle is already on a tower-controlled frequency and local can cancel it later if needed.
Tell the vehicle to monitor local frequency without any coordination because active-runway crossings never require prior approval.
Refuse all active-runway crossings unless local control personally speaks to the vehicle on the local frequency every time.

items.explanation: The rule requires positive, specific coordination before the crossing and a completion advisory afterward.

items.situation: You are authorizing a runway crossing at Taxiway Alpha. Build the crossing instruction.
items.target_phrase: CROSS RUNWAY TWO SEVEN AT ALPHA

items.question_text: As a minimum, what must local and ground controllers exchange for safe and efficient use of runways and movement areas?

choices.text: Aircraft identification and applicable runway, intersection, or taxiway information.
Aircraft identification and the pilot's cruising altitude request only.
The current ATIS code and the last braking-action report only.
Taxi instructions only, because runway information belongs solely to local control.

items.explanation: The paragraph sets that minimum content even though the exchange can occur by several different means.

items.question_text: Ground control must notify local control when a departing aircraft has been taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: A departure taxied to a different runway changes the local controller's operational picture and must be passed along.

items.question_text: Ground control must notify local control of any aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Intersection departures must be passed to local control, whether by verbal means or by flight progress strips.

items.question_text: When the runways in use or the aircraft using them are not visible from the tower or on radar, you must advise the receiving local or ground controller of the aircraft's location before releasing it.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The location report becomes mandatory when the receiving controller cannot otherwise see the aircraft or runway use.

items.situation: An aircraft has been taxied to an intersection departure point on a runway that local cannot see from the tower cab, and the aircraft is also not visible on radar. Another controller says there is no need to pass the aircraft's exact location because the departure strip already moved to local's bay.
items.para_context: Local and ground must exchange aircraft identification and runway, intersection, or taxiway information as needed. When runway use or the aircraft are not visible, the receiving controller must be advised of the aircraft's location before release.

choices.text: Advise local of the aircraft identification, the intersection, and the aircraft's location before releasing control.
Rely on the moved strip alone because physical strip transfer replaces all verbal or written location coordination.
Wait until the aircraft begins takeoff roll, because location coordination is required only after movement starts.
Omit the coordination because intersection-departure information matters only for wake turbulence cases.

items.explanation: When local cannot see the runway use directly, the location advisory is part of the minimum safe coordination.

items.question_text: What must be true before a departure starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold?

choices.text: The runway to be used must be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel.
Only the runway centerline must be clear; vehicles may remain on the shoulders or crossing points if they are moving away.
All vehicles must merely report the runway in sight, even if they are still on it.
The runway may remain occupied if the airport manager has marked the work area with cones and lights.

items.explanation: Before takeoff starts or a landing aircraft crosses the threshold, the runway itself must be free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel.

items.question_text: When established in a letter of agreement, vehicles, equipment, and personnel in two-way communications with ATC may be authorized to operate in the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface, including while aircraft are arriving, departing, or taxiing along the runway.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The LOA authority is for the RSA up to the runway edge, not for unrestricted use of the runway itself.

items.question_text: Ensure that the runway to be used is free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The runway-clear requirement applies to both departures and arrivals.

items.question_text: Proceed as requested may be used to instruct aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel to cross or operate on a runway.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Proceed as requested is not approved phraseology for instructing runway crossing or runway operation.

items.situation: A maintenance truck in two-way communications is covered by an LOA to work in the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface. An arrival is about to cross the threshold, and another controller suggests leaving a second vehicle on the runway a little longer because the truck is already coordinated and both drivers understand the plan.
items.para_context: LOAs may authorize operations in the runway safety area up to the runway edge, but the runway itself must be free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before takeoff starts or an arrival crosses the threshold.

choices.text: Ensure the runway itself is clear before the arrival reaches the threshold, even if LOA-covered work continues only up to the runway edge.
Let the vehicle remain on the runway because LOA authority for the runway safety area also authorizes temporary runway occupancy.
Use "PROCEED AS REQUESTED" to let the vehicle stay on the runway until the arrival is in sight.
Delay the landing clearance requirement only; the runway-clear rule applies to takeoffs but not threshold crossings.

items.explanation: The LOA can cover RSA activity up to the edge, but it does not remove the separate requirement that the runway itself be clear.

items.situation: Airport operations is in two-way communications and requests to work in the runway safety area up to the runway edge, not on the runway. No extra restriction is needed. Build the transmission.

items.question_text: What is the proper basis for airport traffic control service?
items.explanation: Service is based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

choices.text: Observed or known traffic and airport conditions.
Expected traffic flow and the controller's judgment about what is probably happening.
Pilot requests, even when traffic conditions are not known or observed.
Any combination of observed, known, or assumed airport conditions.

items.question_text: Airport traffic control service may be based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.
items.explanation: The paragraph expressly excludes assumptions and unknowns as the basis for service.

items.question_text: Under the note to 3-1-1, who remains responsible for avoiding collision when operating in accordance with the CFRs?
items.explanation: Traffic information may help, but the pilot still bears collision-avoidance responsibility under the CFRs.

choices.text: The pilot.
The local controller exclusively.
The pilot and controller equally under a shared legal duty created by the note.
The airport manager whenever the aircraft is inside the surface area.

items.question_text: Where does the note say traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collision?
items.explanation: The note names Class B, C, and D surface areas, terminal radar service areas, and transiting aircraft near terminal locations.

choices.text: Within Class B, C, or D surface areas, terminal radar service areas, and for transiting aircraft near terminal locations.
Only on final approach inside Class B airspace.
Only when all aircraft involved are IFR and radar identified.
Only after a pilot reports a loss of visual separation.

items.question_text: A controller wants to assure a pilot that unknown traffic is no factor because the pattern is usually predictable. What is correct?
items.explanation: The service basis is limited to observed or known conditions, not assumptions about likely traffic positions.

choices.text: The controller should avoid giving assurance based on assumptions and work only from observed or known conditions.
The controller may assure the pilot as long as the traffic pattern has been stable for several minutes.
The controller must withhold all traffic information because pilots alone are responsible for collision avoidance.
The controller should transfer the aircraft immediately because unknown traffic removes ATC authority to continue service.

items.question_text: When may preventive control service be provided?
items.explanation: Preventive control is limited to LOA-governed operations or DoD equivalents recognized by the note.

choices.text: Only to aircraft operating in accordance with a letter of agreement.
To any aircraft whenever the controller believes routine clearances are unnecessary.
Only during low-traffic periods when the tower can watch the entire airport visually.
Only when the pilot expressly requests it on initial contact.

items.question_text: Preventive control eliminates repetitious routine approval of pilot action.
items.explanation: The note contrasts preventive control with ordinary airport traffic control by removing routine approval language.

items.question_text: When providing preventive control service, when should advice or instructions be issued?
items.explanation: Advice or instructions are reserved for situations that develop and require corrective action.

choices.text: Only if a situation develops that requires corrective action.
At every phase of flight so the pilot knows preventive control is still active.
Only after the pilot requests a control decision.
Whenever two aircraft are operating under the same LOA, even if there is no developing conflict.

items.question_text: DoD airfield operating instructions, memorandums of understanding, or other DoD-only directives may satisfy the agreement requirement for preventive control service.
items.explanation: The note explicitly recognizes those DoD-only directives as satisfying the paragraph's agreement criteria.

items.question_text: A controller is using preventive control for an LOA-covered operation with no developing conflict. What is the correct approach?
items.explanation: Preventive control eliminates repetitive routine approvals and keeps controller intervention for developing conflicts that need correction.

choices.text: Monitor the operation and intervene only if a developing situation requires corrective action.
Issue routine approvals on each leg so the aircraft remains under active preventive control.
Stop all controller involvement because preventive control forbids intervention once the LOA is in effect.
Apply the same reduced-approval technique to any aircraft in the pattern, whether or not it is covered by the LOA.

items.question_text: Who has primary responsibility for operations conducted on the active runway?
items.explanation: The paragraph assigns primary responsibility for active-runway operations to the local controller.

choices.text: The local controller.
The ground controller.
The airport manager.
Whichever controller issued the last taxi instruction.

items.question_text: Ground control must obtain local-control approval before authorizing an aircraft or vehicle to cross or use any portion of an active runway, and the coordination must include the point or intersection involved.
items.explanation: The coordination must be specific to the runway movement and where it will occur.

items.question_text: Complete the runway-crossing authorization: "CROSS RUNWAY TWO SEVEN AT _____"
items.explanation: The authorized phraseology identifies the crossing point or intersection.

choices.text: ALPHA

items.question_text: After a coordinated runway crossing or other coordinated runway operation is complete, what must the ground controller do?
items.explanation: Positive coordination is not complete until local control is advised that the runway operation is finished.

choices.text: Advise the local controller that the coordinated runway operation is complete.
Wait for local control to ask whether the crossing was completed.
Transfer the aircraft or vehicle to advisory frequency so the operator can self-announce completion.
Issue a blanket cancellation of all future runway coordination for that vehicle.

items.question_text: For taxiing or proceeding on or along an active runway for a purpose other than crossing, what is normally required?
items.explanation: Those operations are normally authorized by direct communications on the appropriate local-control frequency, with a narrow facility-directive exception after coordination.

choices.text: Direct communications on the appropriate local-control frequency, unless a facility directive allows ground-frequency authorization after coordination.
Any tower frequency may be used without prior coordination as long as the operator is on two-way communications.
The authorization must always be issued by the airport manager rather than ATC.
Ground control may authorize it on its own frequency whenever the local controller can see the runway.

items.question_text: Before using a runway not previously designated as active, what must the local controller do?
items.explanation: The local controller must coordinate with the ground controller before using a runway that was not previously designated active.

items.question_text: Which methods may local and ground controllers use to exchange runway and movement-area information?
items.explanation: The paragraph allows verbal means, flight progress strips, other written information, or automation displays.

choices.text: Verbal means, flight progress strips, other written information, or automation displays.
Only verbal coordination, because strip or automation transfer does not count as runway coordination.
Only written coordination approved by the airport manager.
Any method except automation displays, which may not be used for controller-to-controller coordination.

items.question_text: Ground control must notify local control when a departing aircraft has been taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active.
items.explanation: That change in departure runway must be passed to local control.

items.question_text: How may notification of an aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff be accomplished?
items.explanation: The paragraph allows either verbal means or flight progress strips for that notification.

choices.text: By verbal means or by flight progress strips.
Only by direct local-control radio contact with the aircraft.
Only by entering the intersection in the ATIS broadcast.
Only by a supervisor-approved handwritten log entry.

items.question_text: When the runways in use or the aircraft using them are not visible from the tower or on radar, the receiving controller must be advised of the aircraft's location before release.
items.explanation: The location advisory becomes part of the required handoff coordination in that visibility-limited case.

items.question_text: An aircraft is taxied to an intersection departure point that local cannot see from the tower cab and that is not visible on radar. What coordination is required before release to local?
items.explanation: The minimum exchange still includes aircraft identification and runway/intersection information, and when visibility is limited the aircraft's location must be passed before release.

choices.text: Advise local of the aircraft identification, the intersection, and the aircraft's location before release.
Move the strip to local and omit further coordination because the strip alone satisfies the requirement.
Wait until takeoff roll begins, because location reports are required only after the aircraft starts moving.
Give only the call sign, because location is unnecessary when the runway assignment has already been coordinated.

items.question_text: When established in a letter of agreement, what may vehicles, equipment, and personnel in two-way communications with ATC be authorized to do?
items.explanation: The LOA authority is limited to the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface.

choices.text: Operate in the runway safety area up to the edge of the runway surface.
Operate anywhere on the runway as long as they remain in two-way communications.
Cross active runways without further ATC authorization once the LOA is signed.
Remain on the runway during arrivals if their vehicles display a flashing beacon.

items.question_text: The runway to be used must be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold.
items.explanation: The runway-clear rule applies at both the start of takeoff and the arrival threshold crossing.

items.question_text: Complete the approved transmission for an LOA-covered vehicle request near the runway edge: "_____ ; and if necessary, additional instructions or information."
items.explanation: The approved phraseology for that RSA-edge authorization is "PROCEED AS REQUESTED."

choices.text: PROCEED AS REQUESTED

items.question_text: Why is "PROCEED AS REQUESTED" not sufficient for runway crossing or runway-operation instructions?
items.explanation: The note says the phrase is not approved for crossing or operating on a runway, so a specific runway authorization must be issued instead.

choices.text: Because it is not approved phraseology for instructing aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel to cross or operate on a runway.
Because it may be used only by airport operations, not by ATC.
Because it is reserved for nonmovement-area work and may never be used near a runway edge.
Because the phrase is approved only when the runway is closed by NOTAM.

items.question_text: "PROCEED AS REQUESTED" may be used to instruct a vehicle to cross or operate on a runway.
items.explanation: That exact use is disapproved by the note.

items.question_text: A vehicle is authorized by LOA to work in the runway safety area up to the runway edge, and a landing aircraft is about to cross the threshold. What is correct?
items.explanation: The LOA may cover RSA-edge operations, but the runway itself must still be clear before the arrival crosses the threshold.

choices.text: The runway itself must be clear of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before the aircraft crosses the threshold.
Any LOA-covered vehicle may remain on the runway as long as it is in two-way communications with ATC.
"PROCEED AS REQUESTED" may be used to keep the vehicle on the runway until the landing aircraft is over the numbers.
The runway-clear requirement applies only to departures, not to landing threshold crossings.
